retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The 12-month and lifetime prevalence is highest in adult men, with 17.6% and 36% respectively. There is a higher prevalence in the White, younger population and in those who were never married or previously married. The lifetime risk for developing DT in the population with alcohol use disorder is approximately 5% to 10%. Risk factors for DT include the following: a history of DT prior history of seizures the presence of concurrent illness with associated comorbidities prior history of detoxification the prolonged period prior to the last drink. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The duration since the last drink becomes critical in recognizing the severity of symptoms. The initial minor withdrawal symptoms are characterized by anxiety, insomnia, palpitations, headache, and gastrointestinal symptoms. These symptoms usually occur as early as 6 hours after cessation of alcohol use. After 12 hours, minor withdrawal symptoms can progress to alcohol hallucinosis, a condition characterized by visual hallucinations. It can typically resolve in 24 to 48 hours and may also be associated with auditory and tactile hallucinations. Alcohol withdrawal seizure is followed by alcohol hallucinosis, though it may present as early as 6 hours before alcohol hallucinosis. It can present as the only withdrawal symptoms but typically resolves 24 to 48 hours later. Seizures can recur, though rarely lead to status epilepticus. Uncharacteristic signs of seizure activity should warrant further workup. If withdrawal symptoms remain untreated, this can typically lead to DT. DT is characterized by visual hallucinations, profound confusion, tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia, agitation, and diaphoresis.

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Delirium tremens occurs in chronic alcohol users who abruptly discontinue alcohol use, often as early as 48 hours. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Delirium tremens was first recognized as a disorder attributed to excessive alcohol use in 1813. It is now commonly known to occur as early as 48 hours after abrupt cessation of alcohol in those with chronic use and can last up to 5 days. It has an anticipated mortality of up to 37% without appropriate treatment. It is crucial to identify early signs of withdrawal because it can become fatal. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians need to evaluate the severity of alcohol withdrawal based on history and clinical presentation. The best-validated tool to assess the severity of alcohol withdrawal is the Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol, Revised (CIWA-Ar). It is a 10-item questionnaire tool to evaluate, monitor, and treat alcohol withdrawal. It includes symptoms of withdrawal such as anxiety, nausea, and sweating, among others. A score of 8 points or lower corresponds to mild withdrawal, while a score of 9 to 15 corresponds to moderate withdrawal, and a score of 15 or greater corresponds to severe withdrawal symptoms, being at risk for seizures and DT. [9] [10] CIWA-Ar is not recommended for withdrawal delirium due to its subjective nature and patients' inability to accurately report withdrawal symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol use disorder is a common condition that has been associated with severe impairments in social functioning and medical problems. As high as 20% of the population have been noted to exhibit alcohol overuse during their lifespan. More than 50% of those with a history of alcohol use disorder can exhibit alcohol withdrawal symptoms at discontinuing or decreasing their alcohol use. However, only a few (3% to 5%) exhibit symptoms of severe alcohol withdrawal with profound confusion, autonomic hyperactivity, and cardiovascular collapse. This is defined as alcohol withdrawal delirium, more commonly known as delirium tremens (DT).

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Mortality from DT has substantially decreased with early recognition and treatment. Early treatment has decreased the mortality rate to less than 5%. Complications from DTs include severe rhabdomyolysis, arrhythmia, and associated comorbid illness. Risk factors such as pneumonia, pancreatitis, older age, and a history of other medical problems can lead to increased mortality.

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Additional evaluation of a patient with DT involves identifying electrolyte, nutrition, and fluid abnormalities. Most of these patients present with severe dehydration (up to 10 L fluid deficit) and severe electrolyte abnormalities, including hypoglycemia, severe hypomagnesemia, and hypophosphatemia. Multivitamins and thiamine should be supplemented before glucose is given to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy. Risk Factors History of DTs Concurrent illness Advanced age Hypokalemia Prior withdrawal symptoms or detoxification

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Recognizing individuals with a history of alcohol use disorder can help prevent the progression of withdrawal symptoms. The US Preventative Services Task Force recommends screening individuals aged 18 or older involved with risky drinking and engaging these individuals with behavior therapy and interventions to decrease alcohol misuse. There is a lack of consensus on the prophylactic treatment of alcohol withdrawal. Variations in hospital-wide policies in treating alcohol withdrawal exist, and the medications used include benzodiazepines and even gabapentin.

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Certain individuals are more vulnerable to developing withdrawal symptoms than others. Though the etiology remains unclear, there is a correlation between the duration of alcohol exposure and withdrawal symptoms. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment goals for alcohol withdrawal are to control agitation, decrease the risk of seizures, and decrease morbidity and mortality. The most common and validated treatment for alcohol withdrawal is benzodiazepine. Several benzodiazepines can be used, including lorazepam, diazepam, and chlordiazepoxide, preferably administered via an intravenous route. This can be administered based on a symptom-triggered regimen or a fixed schedule. In a symptom-triggered regimen, medications are usually given when symptoms are present, sometimes using a CIWA score greater than 8. In a fixed schedule regimen, the benzodiazepine is administered at fixed intervals, and additional doses are given based on the withdrawal symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol also inhibits the action of NMDA receptors by acting as a receptor antagonist. It inhibits the action of glutamate, which is an excitatory amino acid. Prolonged alcohol use results in receptor up-regulation. Abrupt discontinuation of alcohol causes an increase in the action of glutamate, resulting in profound excitatory action. This may have a clinical manifestation of sympathetic overdrive, such as agitation, tremors, tachycardia, and hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The initial history and physical examination are crucial to establish the diagnosis and evaluate the severity of alcohol withdrawal. Pertinent information in the medical history includes quantity and duration of alcohol use, duration since last drink, prior history and severity of alcohol withdrawal, and any additional drug use. Additional information should be identified regarding any complicating medical problems such as heart failure, coronary heart disease, and chronic liver disease, among others. Some of the symptoms may not be self-reported, and as a result, further evaluation is often needed. Physical examination and laboratory testing should be curtailed to identify underlying medical problems and identifying potential electrolyte abnormalities, renal and liver function,  sources of infection, coronary ischemia, rhabdomyolysis, and other drug use.

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: DTs are very common in hospitalized patients and can be difficult to manage. DTs also prolong hospital stays and result in a high cost of healthcare. Today, the emphasis is on the prevention of DTs. To prevent excessive use of alcohol, there are many screening tools available that should be performed in the emergency department or primary care settings. The screening tools are carried out by a nurse or social worker. There is good evidence showing that screening for alcohol use can result in a reduction of alcohol consumption, associated injuries, and a decreased rate of readmissions to hospitals. [13] [14] [15] [Level 5] Nurses also run computer-based programs to screen and counsel patients who have been discharged with a diagnosis of DTs. Data on short-term or acute drinkers do show that these screening protocols work, but whether they work in severe misusers of alcohol remains debatable. Anytime a patient presents with a history of alcohol use, one should try and quantify the use.

retrieved_docs.title: Withdrawal Syndromes -- Epidemiology -- Alcohol
retrieved_docs.content: According to data from the National Epidemiologic Survey on alcohol and related conditions, the estimated lifetime prevalence of alcohol use disorder was 12.8%. [22] Approximately 20% of adults in the emergency room may have alcohol use disorder, and about 4% to 40% of patients admitted to the intensive care unit will have alcohol withdrawal symptoms (AWS). [23] [24] Patients hospitalized with AWS have an increased length of hospital stay and increased mortality compared to patients without AWS. [25] Chronic alcoholism and withdrawal are more common in men than in women. [23] The mortality rate from alcohol withdrawal and delirium tremens is high if untreated, with as many as 5% of these patients developing delirium tremens when they withdraw from chronic alcohol use.

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol acts as a central nervous system depressant. It enhances the effect of inhibitory neurotransmitters while down-regulating excitatory neurotransmitters. Alcohol interacts with GABA receptors, chloride ion receptor acting as an inhibitory neurotransmitter, via several mechanisms to enhance its activity. Over time, through prolonged alcohol exposure, there is a decrease in GABA activity and alteration in the type of GABA receptor and function. Abrupt cessation of alcohol causes a decrease in the inhibitory actions of the GABA neurotransmitter, resulting in overactivity of the central nervous system.

The non‑infectious delirium/brain disorder (SMQ) similarly features apathy (اللامبالاة), attention impairment, reduced concentration, and primary excessive daytime sleepiness. Consequently, excessive daytime sleepiness serves as a linking symptom across arousal disturbances in both encephalopathic and delirium‑like SMQ categories, underscoring its clinical relevance for clinicians managing non‑infectious brain disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 220
retrieved_docs.content: encephalitis spectrum includes the two awakening disorders and is linked to motor and muscular hyperactivity and excessive daytime sleepiness. Likewise, “اللامبالاة” is a component of the delirium/brain‑damage category “الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ),” which also comprises attention deficits, reduced concentration, and excessive daytime sleepiness. Collectively, these interrelated entities depict a heterogeneous network in which depressive, psychotic, substance‑use, encephalitic, and delirium‑related conditions share overlapping clinical features—such as reduced affect, hyperactivity, sleep‑wake disturbances, and cognitive impairments—underscoring the necessity for integrated assessment and personalized management in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 260
retrieved_docs.content: observed in non‑infectious encephalitis (التهاب الدماغ غير المعدي) and non‑infectious delirium/brain dysfunction (الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي), where they co‑occur with motor and muscular hyperactivity and the transient and continuous awakening disorders that affect initiation and maintenance of vigilance. These overlapping symptom patterns underscore the importance of comprehensive assessment across depressive, encephalitic, and delirious presentations to inform accurate diagnosis and individualized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 706
retrieved_docs.content: اللامبالاة (apathy) is identified as a clinical feature of الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ) (delirium/non‑organic brain syndrome). The syndrome itself is classified under خلل في الانتباه (attention disorder) and under ضعف التركيز (concentration impairment). Thus, attention deficits and reduced concentration are core aspects of delirium/non‑organic brain syndrome, and apathy may appear as one of its manifestations. Recognizing these interrelated signs supports more accurate assessment and management of patients with cognitive and attentional disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 0 -- Community 2
retrieved_docs.content: “non‑infectious encephalitis” SMQ, connecting sleep‑wake dysregulation to inflammatory brain processes. Apathy belongs to the “non‑infectious delirium/brain disease” SMQ, which additionally includes attention‑deficit, concentration weakness and early daytime sleepiness, linking cognitive‑motor deficits with delirium‑type brain disease. Gender identity is associated with stereotyped repetitive movements; together with emotional blunting, these signs are interpreted as clinical expressions of localized brain atrophy. Miscellaneous mental‑health disorders are broadly related to an unspecified transient organic mental disorder and are primarily mapped from organic mood disorder, indicating an organic substrate for diverse psychiatric presentations. A confusional state without acute features and an organic hallucination syndrome are members of the “anticholinergic syndrome” SMQ, reflecting anticholinergic toxicity as a cause of acute psychotic‑like states. Schizophrenic mania (“hous

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 144
retrieved_docs.content: Behavioral/psychiatric manifestations are organized under the broader concept of الهَذَيان (delirium), and الهَذَيان in turn includes the specific category حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة (transient organic delirium cases). Both التهاب الدماغ غير المعدي (SMQ) (non‑infectious encephalitis) and الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ) (delirium/non‑infectious brain disorder) are SMQ groupings that list حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة as members. Within التهاب الدماغ غير المعدي (SMQ), the following clinical features are identified as members: الاضطراب العابر في بدء الاستيقاظ أو المحافظة عليه (transient disorder of sleep onset or maintenance), الاضطراب المستمر في بدء الاستيقاظ أو استمراره (continuous disorder of sleep onset or maintenance), فرط النشاط الحركي (hyperkinetic activity), فرط النشاط العضلي (muscle hyperactivity), and فرط النوم في النهار الأولي (primary excessive daytime sleepiness). In the related grouping الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ), the entities اللامبالاة (apathy), خلل في الانتباه (attention deficit), ضعف التركيز (poor concentration), and فرط النوم في النهار الأولي (primary excessive daytime sleepiness) are listed as members. This hierarchical structure shows how delirium‑related behavioral and psychiatric manifestations are linked to specific transient organic delirium presentations and how those presentations are further categorized under distinct non‑infectious encephalitic and delirium brain‑disorder SMQ clusters, each characterized by particular sleep‑wake disturbances, motor abnormalities, and cognitive‑affective symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 64
retrieved_docs.content: self‑destructive behavior, increased vigilance, alcohol poisoning, marijuana use, mood swings, attention and memory disturbances, psychological distress, weak concentration, reduced affect, motor hyperactivity (فرط النشاط الحركي), muscular hyperactivity (فرط النشاط العضلي) and excessive daytime sleepiness (فرط النوم في النهار الأولي). The non‑infectious encephalitis syndrome (التهاب الدماغ غير المعدي (SMQ)) encompasses transient and continuous wake‑onset disorders and is associated with the hyperkinetic manifestations listed above. The non‑infectious delirium/brain dysfunction syndrome (الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ)) is characterized by attention disorder, weak concentration and excessive daytime sleepiness, and includes apathy as a hallmark feature. Early‑onset (infancy) disorders are related to unspecified childhood psychosis, indicating that early psychotic phenomena are part of this broader diagnostic network. Overall, the model emphasizes the overlap between organic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2270
retrieved_docs.content: Organic mental disorder caused by psychoactive substance is a broad diagnostic category that includes several specific subtypes. Among these, Sedative, hypnotic AND/OR anxiolytic intoxication delirium represents a form of delirium that arises from the acute intoxication of depressant substances such as sedatives, hypnotics, or anxiolytics. Another distinct subtype is intoxicatie; hallucinogeen, which denotes mental disorder manifestations resulting from the acute intoxication of hallucinogenic agents. Recognizing that both Sedative, hypnotic AND/OR anxiolytic intoxication delirium and intoxicatie; hallucinogeen are child categories of Organic mental disorder caused by psychoactive substance helps clinicians differentiate the underlying substance class, anticipate characteristic clinical features, and tailor appropriate treatment and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 145
retrieved_docs.content: apathy, and is grouped together with non‑infectious encephalitis and psychosis within a broader neuropsychiatric framework. Conduct disorder is described as encompassing non‑infectious encephalitis, psychosis, and delirium. Collectively, the knowledge base highlights extensive overlap among depressive, substance‑use, psychotic, encephalitic, and delirious processes, emphasizing the need for comprehensive assessment of affect, cognition, motor activity, sleep‑wake regulation, and substance‑related factors in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: hypersomnia. Indifference belongs to the non‑infectious delirium/brain pathology SMQ, which likewise includes attention deficits, poor concentration and early‑day hypersomnia. Repetitive stereotypic movements and emotional numbness are presented as manifestations of localized brain atrophy “ضمور المخ المحدد.” Organic psychosis is linked to schizophrenic mania, reflecting an intersection between organic mental disorders and schizophrenic patterns. Withdrawal and relapse phenomena feature recurrent tachycardia, underscoring that cardiac symptoms can be part of substance‑withdrawal syndromes. Collectively, these interlinked categories and relationships constitute a comprehensive, multilayered classification system that integrates anxiety, mood, psychotic, developmental, neuro‑organic, psychosexual, substance‑use, and gender‑identity disorders, supporting precise diagnosis, nuanced understanding of comorbidity, and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 100
retrieved_docs.content: excessive daytime sleepiness. Delirium/non‑inflammatory brain pathology includes apathy (اللامبالاة), attention disorder, concentration weakness, and excessive daytime sleepiness. The term ذُهان عضوي describes mental disorders with an organic or biological basis and subsumes a broader class of organic psychotic disorders. Within this class, هوس شَبَقِيّ is identified as a specific type of delusional mania that exhibits psychotic‑like features and is classified as an organic psychotic disorder. These hierarchical relationships illustrate how mental‑symptom domains intersect with organic psychotic conditions, how diverse depressive phenotypes are organized under a unified depression framework, and how substance‑use, behavior, psychotic, and neuro‑behavioral manifestations are integrated to support comprehensive clinical assessment and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 128
retrieved_docs.content: أو استمراره, فرط النشاط الحركي, فرط النشاط العضلي, فرط النوم في النهار الأولي, اللامبالاة, خلل في الانتباه, and ضعف التركيز, characterizing non‑infectious encephalopathy. Excessive daytime sleepiness (فرط النوم في النهار الأولي) is classified as a manifestation of non‑organic delirium/brain dysfunction (الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي SMQ), whereas organic delirium (ذُهان عضوي) shows a broader relationship with هوس شَبَقِيّ, indicating that organic and non‑organic forms of delirium are linked to distinct clinical presentations yet both intersect with broader psychotic disorders. These hierarchical relationships illustrate how mental symptoms, organic and non‑organic psychotic states, attention‑deficit, conduct disorders, a wide array of depressive subtypes, childhood psychoses, and neurobehavioral/sleep abnormalities are organized within the diagnostic framework, supporting accurate assessment and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 0 -- Community 21
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s taxonomy arranges mental‑health and neuro‑behavioral conditions by organic, developmental, pharmacologic and sexual‑health etiologies. “اضطراب القلق بسبب حالة طبية عامة” (anxiety disorder due to a general medical condition) includes the subtypes “Anxiety disorder caused by drug” and anxiety attributable to known physiological conditions; both are classified under the broader “miscellaneous mental health disorders” group, indicating that drug‑induced and physiologically based anxiety are secondary to medical illness. “اضطراب الحالة المزاجية العضوي” (organic mood disorder) captures mood disturbances with an identifiable organic cause, such as drug‑induced mood changes, and is likewise linked to “miscellaneous mental health disorders”. Delirium (“الهَذَيان”) is a member of the “متلازمة مُضَادّات الفِعْلِ الكولينيّ (SMQ)” (cholinergic antagonist syndrome) and is a child of “حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة” (transient organic psychotic states). This node also parents “التهاب

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 79
retrieved_docs.content: encephalopathy spectrum, apathy (اللامبالاة) is a component of the delirium/brain‑dysfunction syndrome (الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ)), which also encompasses attention deficits, reduced concentration and the same early‑stage excessive daytime sleepiness. Organic psychosis (ذُهان عضوي) shows a broader relationship with obsessive‑compulsive disorder (هوس شَبَقِيّ), suggesting overlapping pathophysiology. Overall, the model emphasizes the interdependence of depressive symptomatology, psychotic and neuroinflammatory conditions, attention‑deficit and conduct disorders, and substance‑withdrawal phenomena, underscoring the need for integrated assessment and coordinated multidisciplinary care.

Within the psychosis SMQ, mood‑related diagnoses such as recurrent and single‑episode major depressive episodes, melancholic depression (اكتئاب أَوبِيّ), mood lability, and mania (هوس شَبَقِيّ) are also members, highlighting overlap among depressive, manic and psychotic features. The non‑infectious encephalitis SMQ includes the two awakening‑related disorders, indicating that arousal disturbances may manifest as encephalitic processes. Non‑infectious encephalitis is characterized by hyperkinetic motor activity, increased muscular activity and pronounced early‑phase excessive daytime sleepiness. In the broader non‑infectious encephalopathy spectrum, apathy (اللامبالاة) is a component of the delirium/brain‑dysfunction syndrome (الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ)), which also encompasses attention deficits, reduced

The classification “diseases and injuries” includes malaise and fatigue, mapped to feeling of weakness (الشعور بالضعف), fatigue (النوام), and broader neurogenic weakness (الضعف العصبي). Infant‑onset disorders are connected to unspecified active childhood psychosis (ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة), a member of the SMQ group of psychotic and schizophrenic disorders (الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ)). The SMQ group also contains depressive and mood disorders listed above and is a member of blunted affect (عاطفة متقلصة). Non‑infectious encephalitis (التهاب الدماغ غير المعدي (SMQ)) includes transient awakening disorders, while non‑infectious encephalopathy (التهاب الدماغ غير المعدي (SMQ)) comprises continuous wakefulness disturbances, hyperkinetic activity, muscle hyperactivity, and primary excessive daytime sleepiness.
